prompt: John Adams argues that the Quasi-War with France demonstrated the necessity of building a powerful naval force and expanding the regular army, and that America must match European military establishments to be taken seriously as a nation. He criticizes the Republicans for leaving the country defenseless. Mr. Madison, what is the proper scale of American military establishment in peacetime?
ground_truth_signal: Madison favored a modest regular military with reliance on state militias as the primary defense force, supplemented by a small professional navy for coastal and commercial defense. Unlike Adams and Hamilton who sought forces comparable to European establishments, Madison believed large peacetime military forces were fiscally unsustainable for a republic and dangerous to civil liberties. He prioritized a small but professional officer corps, improved militia organization, and coastal fortifications over a large standing army. The War of 1812 chastened him somewhat regarding militia reliability but did not fundamentally change his preference for limited regular forces over European-scale military establishments.
response:
Mr. Adams speaks from the honest conviction of a man who witnessed our humiliation at the hands of French privateers, and I do not question his patriotism or his concern for the national honor. Yet I must respectfully dissent from his conclusions, for they rest upon premises that I believe to be both dangerous to republican liberty and unsuited to our peculiar circumstances as a nation.

The question before us is not whether America requires the means of defense — no reasonable man disputes this necessity. The question is whether we shall model our military establishment upon the pattern of European monarchies, with their standing armies and vast naval armaments, or whether we shall devise a system more consonant with republican principles and the genius of our people.

First, let us consider the nature of our situation. We are separated from the great military powers of Europe by three thousand miles of ocean — a barrier more formidable than any fortress that human art has yet contrived. Our immediate neighbors are weak, and our internal frontiers, though extensive, face no enemy capable of mounting a serious invasion of our heartland. Why then should we burden ourselves with military establishments designed to meet threats that geography itself renders improbable?

Second, experience has shown us that standing armies in time of peace are dangerous to the liberties of a free people. They consume the public treasure, they create interests hostile to economy and peace, and they habituate the government to the use of force in its domestic as well as its foreign relations. The armies of Europe exist not merely to defend their nations against external enemies, but to maintain their kings upon their thrones against the discontent of their own subjects. We have no such need, and we ought not to create the instruments that might someday serve such a purpose.

Third, the expense of maintaining military forces upon the European scale would require taxes that our people will not long endure. Mr. Adams speaks of being taken seriously by foreign nations, but what nation can be thought serious that bankrupts itself in the pursuit of military glory? The true strength of America lies not in our capacity to match the armaments of despotic governments, but in our prosperity, our growing population, and the attachment of our citizens to their free institutions.

I do not counsel defenselessness. A modest naval force, sufficient to protect our commerce and coasts, is both necessary and proper. A small regular army, adequate to garrison our frontier posts and serve as the nucleus for expansion in time of war, serves the same purpose. But the principal reliance of a free people must be upon their militia — citizens who take up arms in defense of their homes and return to their peaceable occupations when the danger has passed.

The militia system possesses advantages that no standing army can claim. It is economical, requiring no expense in time of peace beyond the cost of arms and occasional training. It is republican, drawing its officers from the people themselves rather than from a separate military caste. It is defensive in its very nature, unsuited to wars of conquest or aggression. And it provides a check upon the ambitions of government, for no administration can employ citizen-soldiers against their own liberties with the same facility it might employ mercenaries.

Mr. Adams fears that foreign nations will not respect a republic that appears weak. I fear more that they will not respect a republic that abandons its principles in the pursuit of their good opinion. The European powers maintain their vast military establishments because they live in a state of perpetual jealousy and preparation for war. Their governments rest upon force, and force is the language they understand. But America was founded upon different principles — upon the consent of the governed, upon the peaceful resolution of disputes, upon commerce rather than conquest as the means of national prosperity.

If we are to be taken seriously by the world, let it be as a nation that has discovered how free men may govern themselves without the apparatus of despotism. Let our strength be found not in our capacity to destroy, but in our capacity to create — not in the fear we inspire, but in the example we provide.

The Quasi-War, which Mr. Adams cites as proof of our military inadequacy, seems to me to demonstrate rather the opposite. We defended our commerce, we maintained our honor, and we avoided the ruinous expense of a general war — all without transforming ourselves into a military nation. This appears to me a success, not a failure, of republican policy.

I would not leave the country defenseless, but neither would I sacrifice its republican character upon the altar of military ambition. A free people, fighting in defense of their own soil, are worth more than twice their number of hired soldiers fighting for pay. Let us trust in this truth, and in the protection that Providence has afforded us through our geographical situation, rather than burden ourselves with establishments that have proved the ruin of liberty in every age and nation where they have been tried.
